hypotheses: Individuals under mortality salience are less likely to give utilitarian responses to moral conflicts.
claim.statement: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian.

data.source: Participants recruited on campus at the University of Toulouse
data.notes: Experiment 2 involved three levels of cognitive load manipulation.

method.description: Two experiments were conducted to investigate the effect of mortality salience on utilitarian moral judgment.
steps: Participants were randomly assigned to conditions.
Mortality salience was manipulated by having participants think about death.
Participants judged moral acceptability of actions in scenarios.
Experiment 2 included varying cognitive loads before participants assessed scenarios.
method.independent_variables: Mortality salience condition, cognitive load levels
method.control_variables: Pain condition as control for aversive emotions

results.summary: Mortality salience decreased utilitarian responses in moral conflicts, comparable to very high cognitive load.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Utilitarian response frequency under mortality salience
numerical_results.p_value: p = .003 for conﬂict problems under mortality salience

metadata.original_paper_title: Mortality salience and morality: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian
